watch_out: Extremely loose and unstable rock throughout descent—Mount Stuart granite is heavily jointed and known for rockfall; take great care navigating loose rock on the final traverse above the headwall
Sparse protection on P6 corner crack section—secure all placements before committing to exposed traverses
Exposed horizontal crack traverse (P3 'Monkey Traverse')—significant runout if protection fails; requires confident climbing and solid anchor placements
Rope management difficulties on P5 and P6 from wandering pitch terrain—ensure smooth feeding and clearing of potential snags
Descent via western ridge gully and single-rope rappel from fixed slings—inspect anchor quality before committing weight; loose rock accumulation in gully
Route-finding complexity on lower pitches—verify color-marked bolts and cairns in approach to avoid losing the route on granite slabs
Afternoon thunderstorms common in summer—start early (before 7am) and monitor weather; retreat if storms approach
Berkschrund crossing at base of climbers' trail—may require downclimbing on snow/ice; assess conditions carefully in spring/early summer

CRITICAL AVALANCHE TERRAIN: Slope angle of 35-45 degrees with extensive vertical relief and frequent terrain traps—couloir is prime avalanche slope with multiple fatal accidents (3 deaths in Feb 2023); only climb in ideal snow conditions with confirmed low/minimal avalanche hazard
Entry and exit of steep couloir can be technical with snow or ice up to 60 degrees—difficult down-climbing if route-finding error occurs; requires excellent snow/ice judgment
Rock and ice fall from walls above couloir—stay alert and compact party; position out of direct fall line; watch for ice/rock loosening due to solar warming
Bergschrund crossing at glacier base—may be significant obstacle early/mid-season with potential fall hazard; probe and belay crossing if uncertain
Late-season glacier becomes bare ice (July+)—surface becomes extremely difficult and icy; crampons and ice tools essential; increased rockfall risk from warming
Crevasses in upper Colchuck Glacier approach (though fewer than on other glacier routes)—travel roped; probe continuously
Limited escape options once committed to upper couloir—retreat can be hazardous in poor conditions; plan to turn back early if conditions deteriorate
Navigation difficulty in whiteout conditions—few visual landmarks; GPS/map essential; descending in low visibility is particularly risky
Extremely loose and occasionally mossy rock throughout entire buttress—significant rockfall hazard; secure all placements and test holds before committing weight
Tricky route-finding, especially around reported missing left-facing dihedral—easy to wander off established line onto worse rock; requires careful study of topo before climb
A marathon 17-21 hour car-to-car commitment—lengthy exposure to weather and objective hazards; start pre-dawn and maintain continuous forward progress
Crossing moat and bergschrund at buttress base—can be significant, especially early season; probe carefully and belay uncertain crossings
Exposed rock pitches in mid-buttress section—runouts between protection on mossy/dirty rock; climbing quality variable; conservative rope placement essential
Descent complexity via rappels and down-climbing—route-finding can be difficult in darkness; headlamps on both climbers recommended; pre-plan rappel anchors
Potential for afternoon thunderstorms—start very early to reduce exposure to weather in exposed upper buttress
High altitude exposure (8700+ft) with sustained climbing—altitude sickness possible; acclimatization and hydration essential on this long push
Limited bailout options once committed past mid-buttress—retreat becomes increasingly difficult and complex; commit only in good conditions

SIGNIFICANT ROCKFALL HAZARD from above and within approach gully—signs of rockfall damage visible; gully floor contains numerous 10-50 pound rocks precariously balanced in fine sand; traverse quickly and watch continuously for falling rocks

Critical route-finding to identify correct approach gully—documented cases of parties entering wrong gully, leading to complex traverses and tragic accidents (2005 incident with 3 fatalities); study photos and topos carefully; may want to hire guide for first ascent

Exposed 5.10 alpine rock climbing with limited protection in some sections—high run-out potential on compact slab pitches; requires confident climbing and solid judgment on when to place protection vs. accept exposure
Rappel anchor quality variable—fixed slings/bolts wear over time and may have reduced holding power; always back up rappel anchors or build new ones; inspect carefully before committing full weight
Gully system loose and unstable—rockfall debris accumulation indicates ongoing hazard; potential for rockfall triggered by climbing party or weather changes; stay aware of partner locations

Weather exposure on open face—afternoon clouds common; thunderstorm potential in summer; electric storms with rock climbing exposure is extremely dangerous; start early and retreat if weather deteriorates

Descending gully in darkness—route-finding becomes difficult without daylight; headlamps not fully sufficient for gully route-finding; strongly consider camping at base if climb extends past early afternoon
Altitude and sustained climbing (5000+ vertical from trailhead)—technical grade combined with elevation can cause dehydration and altitude effects; start well-hydrated and maintain energy intake
Loose rock throughout entire route—Mount Stuart area granite is heavily jointed and unstable; continuous rockfall hazard; test all holds and placements; wear helmet at all times
Route-finding complexity on ridge sections—exposed traverses can be confusing in poor visibility; study topo and photos before climb; carries run-out risk if wrong line taken

Afternoon thunderstorm exposure on open ridges—peak elevation and exposed position make this dangerous with afternoon lightning; start very early (before sunrise) to maximize summit time before storm potential

Unprotectable 5.6 slab move to true summit with single old 1/4-inch bolt—bolt is unreliable and many parties don't trust it; requires confident climbing or alternative descent if unwilling to commit; potential runout of 50+ feet to exposed terrain
Approach gullies hold snow into July (early season hazard)—snow/ice may require ice axe; late spring descent can be icy and slippery; microspikes recommended April-June
Descent route confusion possible—multiple options for descent gully; stay oriented to correct line; potential for glissading out of control if descent gully selection error

Exposed ridge sections with significant consequences—wind exposure and large drops; low-angle scrambling demands respect and caution; not as technical as rating suggests but requires solid mountain sense
Remote location with long approach—marathon day; start pre-dawn and maintain continuous progress; fatigue late in day can lead to mistakes on exposed terrain

Highly condition-dependent climb—seasonal snowfall, freeze-thaw cycles, and sun exposure create variable ice quality; consult current NWAC Snoqualmie Pass forecast and recent trip reports before committing
Ephemeral ice formation—flow may be weak or absent depending on winter conditions; early season (Dec-Jan) typically best; February-March often poor due to sun exposure; April-May risky with weak ice
Rappel anchors on descent are tree-based and wear over time—inspect slings and bolts carefully; always back up anchors; worn gear can fail under body weight on rappel
Start very early given limited winter daylight—short days mean climbers often descending in darkness; headlamp essential; poor visibility increases rappel hazards and route-finding difficulty
Top-down sun exposure melts ice during day—afternoon climbing/descent significantly more dangerous; anchors may fail if ice is actively melting; ice quality degrades rapidly in afternoon
Weather exposure on open slab—wind loading on exposed aspect; afternoon clouds common; thunderstorm potential even in winter; retreat if weather deteriorates
Technical ice climbing on slab angle—80+ degree ice requires solid mixed climbing skills; poor ice conditions create runout risk; sustained climbing intensity over 3-4 hours tiring
Crevasse danger in lower approach terrain—though typically minor, glacier patches and seasonal crevasses possible; rope up if uncertain of terrain; probe continuously in whiteout conditions
Rotten rock and rockfall hazard throughout upper peak—granite and metamorphic rock severely weathered; continuous hazard from loosening rocks; stay alert and compact; helmet mandatory
Exposed chimney/roof pitch on upper pitches—significant protection challenge on friable rock; runout potential if placements fail; requires confident climbing and careful anchor placement
Navigation to correct NE summit complicated by multiple peaks—wrong summit selection can add hours and difficulty; study photos and use GPS; false summits create fatigue and disorientation

Steep snow and small glacier high on route—transition from rock to snow/ice can be hazardous; assess conditions and have appropriate tools (ice axe, crampons); late season bare ice or crevasse exposure
Crystal Pass crossing—bergschrund potential; late season route-finding confusion; always probe and belay uncertain snow/ice crossings
Altitude exposure with sustained climbing—7700+ feet with continuous elevation gain; altitude sickness possible; acclimatization and hydration essential
Weather exposure on open ridge traverse—afternoon storms common in Olympics; exposed position dangerous with lightning; start pre-dawn
Descent terrain equally demanding—route-finding on descent must-do; darkness increases risk; maintain focus on descent line throughout climb

Morning snow in couloir can be firm or icy and masks how technical the underlying rock actually is—assess and re-assess snow conditions; if rock climbing is required, retreat if not capable; snow/ice transition can be slippery and dangerous

Crux rock step (20-50 ft above saddle) more serious than Class 3-4 billing suggests—at least one party ended up roping up and placing gear due to steepness and poor rock quality; secure ropes on this section unless climbing with extreme confidence

Don't glissade descent chute without clear sightline to runout—nearby terrain has close calls from parties glissading blind into rocks or drop-offs; walking descent safer than sliding in poor visibility
Couloir/gully approach terrain—potential for rockfall from upper sections; stay compact and move through quickly; afternoon rockfall increase from sun warming; start early
Snow conditions seasonal and highly variable—spring (May-June) typically best; summer dry conditions expose loose rock; winter requires mountaineering skills; verify current conditions
Route-finding complexity on initial scramble approach—establish correct line before committing; terrain can look similar but lead to worse ground
Moat crossing potential at upper couloir sections—bergschrund may be significant; assess before committing; belay if uncertain
Fatigue on descent—long day with technical climbing can leave climbers tired; descents often cause more injuries; maintain focus and move deliberately on all scrambling

Moat crossing at base of ridge—glacier/snowfield undercuts ridge base creating gap; late season moats can be 5-10+ feet deep; probe and belay crossing carefully; early season smaller but may have bergschrund
Glacier crevasses on approach and lower sections—continuous roping essential; probe every step; crevasse rescue preparation required for solo/small parties
Worn slings at rappel anchor—fixed gear degrades over time; inspect carefully and consider backing up anchor or building new one; always test anchor before committing full weight to rappel
Exposed ridge climbing with significant consequences—sustained scrambling on exposed ridge with large drop-offs; no protection on many sections; confident climbing and solid mountain sense essential
Weather exposure on high ridge—exposed to wind and afternoon storms; lightning potential on exposed sections; start early and retreat if weather deteriorates
Route-finding complexity on ridge sections—terrain confusing in poor visibility; easy to wander off established line; study topo before climbing
Descent rappels in poor weather—limited visibility increases accident risk; headlamps necessary; poor lighting complicates anchor location and rope management
Altitude and sustained approach—7000+ foot glacier approach with continuous elevation gain; hydration and energy management critical

Sustained 5.9/5.10- friction and seam climbing with real runouts between bolts on slab pitches—protection strategy critical; runout potential of 20+ feet on some pitches if protection fails; requires bold climbing and good bolt reading
Route-finding through short connector pitch (P4)—easy to miss or take variant that increases difficulty; study topo and photos; clear marking or experience essential to identify correct line
Slab exposure on upper pitches—significant consequence if protection fails; route-finding errors can lead to unprotectable climbing; thorough topo study mandatory
Weather exposure on open northeast face—afternoon storm potential; exposed position dangerous with lightning; wind common on slab sections; start early and monitor weather
Approach terrain loose and exposed—scrambling on granite requires caution; early season snow/ice possible on approach; late season loose rock hazard
Descent via rappels and down-climbing—route-finding important; terrain can be confusing in darkness; headlamps required; inspect anchor quality carefully
Altitude and sustained climbing—8000+ feet with continuous elevation; altitude sickness possible; hydration and energy management critical
Friable rock on some sections—typical North Cascades granite; test holds and placements; helmet recommended due to rockfall potential from above
Ephemeral waterfall ice formation—flow dependent on consistent freezing temperatures and water source; inconsistent conditions season-to-season; may not form or may be fragile
Diurnal freeze-thaw cycles determine quality—afternoon warming melts ice; evening refreezing creates variable conditions; early morning climbing critical for optimal ice
Avalanche terrain exposure—upper drainage may load with wind slab; assess stability before committing to climb; wide gully presents potential for slide if conditions unstable
Falling ice hazard from above—ice deterioration and calving risk as temperatures rise; position carefully and move quickly through gully; afternoon climbing extremely hazardous
Steep ice pitches (80+ degrees) with protection challenges—quality ice and anchor placement critical; poor conditions = unprotectable climbing with high consequence
Route-finding in whiteout conditions—gully system can be confusing in poor visibility; GPS/map essential; easy to enter wrong drainage
Limited bailout options once committed—terrain traps in lower gully; retreat can be difficult; commit only in established conditions
Approach hazards including avalanche exposure—approach gully subject to slides; assess slope stability; time approach carefully in safe hours
Ultra-ephemeral ice formation—even less consistent than Iceman; highly dependent on unusual weather patterns; often forms only in coldest, snowiest years
Water source limited and variable—snowmelt dependency means formation marginal in warm years; verify conditions from recent reports before committing
Highly condition-dependent climbing—ice quality can range from nil to excellent depending on recent weather; check NWAC/CascadeClimbers reports before approach
Technical grade masks variable protection—WI3-4 rating assumes quality ice; poor ice conditions create unprotectable runouts with serious consequences
Afternoon deterioration extreme—late morning climbing risky; even slight warming weakens ice; must climb in pre-dawn cold hours only
Significant avalanche exposure in approach—gully terrain subject to slides; assess stability; early start essential for safe approach
Approach terrain variable and sometimes snow-choked—route-finding difficult; terrain traps in gullies; potential for slide; microspikes/crampons needed
Limited climbing window in season—typically Dec-Jan only in most years; highly weather-dependent; low probability of climbable conditions
Occasionally loose rock throughout—typical of Dikes granite; variable rock quality requires cautious climbing; always test holds and placements
No continuous crack systems—protection limited and often minimal; runouts between protection; bold climbing required
Exposed arete climbing—significant consequence if protection fails; narrow ridge exposure with drops on both sides; maintains climber focus requirement
Dirty/mossy sections possible—rock quality variable season-to-season; lichen growth and moisture affect climbing texture; clean before climbing if needed
Route-finding complexity on arete variations—multiple lines possible; easy to wander off established route; study topo and photos
Weather exposure on open ridge—afternoon thunderstorms common in eastern Cascades; exposed to wind and lightning; start early
Descent route-finding—rappel anchor locations must be confirmed; two-rope descent may be necessary; descent terrain exposed
Lower altitude but exposed position—7000+ ft elevation gain from trailhead; sustained climbing with exposure; fatigue on descent increases risk
Glacier approach with crevasse field—continuous rope essential; probe every step; bergschrund crossing required at ridge base
Moat crossing at ridge base—seasonal variation; late season moats can be 5-10+ feet with difficult crossing; assess before committing
Loose rock sections on east ridge—unstable granite typical of area; test holds; rockfall potential from upper sections and other climbers
Route-finding on initial ridge sections—terrain confusing with multiple false summits; verify correct line; study photos and topo
Weather exposure on high ridge—exposed position with altitude gain to 8400 ft; afternoon storms common; lightning hazard
Descent complexity—rappel setup required on some sections; route-finding important in descent; tired climbers increase risk
Sustained high-altitude objective—7000+ ft elevation gain to base; hydration and energy management critical; altitude sickness possible
Potential avalanche exposure on approach slopes—assess stability in spring; afternoon warming increases risk; early start essential
Route-finding complexity—alpine rock climbing with terrain variations; easy to wander off established line; study topo and photos
Loose and friable rock—typical Cascades granite; test holds and placements; helmet essential
Exposed scrambling on upper sections—significant consequences if climbing protection fails; runout potential between bolts
Weather exposure on open ridge—afternoon storms common; exposed position dangerous with lightning; start early
Descent route-finding—rappel and down-climbing required; terrain confusing in poor light; headlamp essential
Altitude exposure—7200+ feet; sustained climbing; altitude sickness possible; hydration management critical
Limited bailout options once committed to upper pitches—terrain becomes more serious as altitude increases; commit only in good conditions
Glacier/snow approach possible—seasonal variation; microspikes may be needed; assess conditions before approach
Three large chockstones requiring dry tooling and technical mixed climbing skills; ice coating on rocks creates variable grading
Significant cornice at top requiring careful navigation with multiple bypass options; cornice collapse risk during warm spells
Steep snow pitches 40-65 degrees throughout; loose snow and slough avalanches on gully walls, particularly during spring warming
Limited seasonal window (April-early May after pass opens); narrow timeframe when route is in condition before rapid snowmelt
Mixed terrain transitions between ice, snow, and rock climbing; protection limited on rock sections
Moderate exposure on approach and ascent; falls could be fatal on steeper pitches
Significant avalanche danger on approach; avoid summer trail during winter (high hazard zone); use east face approach to Source Lake
Steep slab climbing with mixed grade M4-5 sections; requires solid mixed climbing ability on exposed terrain
Second pitch features long run-outs on thin ice with difficult screw/picket protection; high consequence if placements fail
Balancy mixed terrain over exposed east face; slipping on slab sections over significant drops
Rime ice buildup on exposed pitches; weather deterioration common in Snoqualmie Pass area
Corniced terrain on descent; rappel hazards with potential rime/ice accumulation on anchor points
Spring avalanche hazard on steep snow couloir (50+ degree slope angle); early season ascents have highest risk during rapid warming cycles
Early seasonal snowmelt limits climbing window to weeks after snow access opens; rapid transitions between stable and unstable snow conditions
Remote alpine cirque location with limited escape options; terrain trap if avalanche occurs; 45-minute approach from trailhead
650-foot vertical exposure through narrow couloir; rockfall and icefall hazard from cliffs above; 750-foot near-vertical rock walls above main couloir
Highly variable conditions dependent on seasonal snowpack; reconnaissance needed to assess current slide activity before committing
Steep northwest-facing gully avalanche terrain; entire route on avalanche-prone NW face; proximity to Phantom Slide zone adds risk
Highly variable ice coverage; recent reports indicate route can be completely dry with minimal ice requiring only 1-2 screw placements total
Complex approach through thick tree coverage and steep snowed-up cliff bands; navigation difficulty without prior knowledge or established skin track
WI3-WI4+ ice climbing with mixed A1-2 aid sections; sustained steep terrain with potential icefall from ice bulges
Steep snowed-up cliffs on NW face create objective hazard from falling ice and cornices; wind-loaded terrain on N and W aspects
Route finding challenges in poor visibility; 4-6 pitch climb with potential for becoming off-route in whiteout conditions
Poor rock quality on winter ascent makes rock sections dangerous; wet and icy rock creates slick conditions and unreliable protection
Multiple fatal fall hazards identified in trip reports; unprotected sections and exposure over steep terrain; fall consequences are serious
Significant icefall hazard from sun exposure; sun hits gully creating dangerous ice fall conditions (ice chunks descending mid-climb)
Blue ice with wet snow accumulation creating poor foot placement; sections near vertical requiring dual-tool technique
Short seasonal window due to low elevation (5,700 ft) and west-facing aspects; very limited periods with stable conditions
12-pitch alpine route with complex rappel descent; icefall risk during descent in daylight-constrained winter season
Avalanche terrain throughout; slopes suitable for slab formation (30-45 degree gully angles below near-vertical sections)
Highly conditions-dependent difficulty; mixed climbing (M4) pitches vary from easy snow/ice to sustained steep sections based on current conditions
Steep ice bulge (A2-3 rating) on pitch 3 with notoriously difficult anchor placement; anchors unreliable and sparingly available above pitch 3
Heavily corniced rappel descent; cornice collapse risk during warm spells; potential for rappel line being cut by cornices
S-shaped gully on pitch 1 with variable mixed climbing up to M4; conditions-dependent route difficulty affects protection options
Long pitch to base of ice bulge (70-80m) on easy terrain, but terrain becomes avalanche-prone on wider snow slopes
Exposed approach traverse; wind-loaded terrain near Snoqualmie Pass; rapid weather changes common
Multiple rappel anchors required for descent; deteriorating conditions during descent can create urgent retreat scenarios
Significant avalanche danger on approach to The Tooth; avoid summer path entirely due to high avalanche exposure
Second pitch protection difficulties; long run-outs on thin ice with inadequate screw/picket placement options; high consequences for protection failure
Balancy mixed slab climbing requiring precise footwork; exposed low 5th class/M3 pitch over east face; 50-70 degree ice sections
Early season ascents face steeper avalanche risk on approach gully; spring warming creates slough and slab hazards
Rime ice accumulation on exposed upper pitches reduces friction and increases slipping risk; direct north aspect can produce rime ice in winter storms
Weather exposure on approach valley; compressed daylight window for winter climbs increases risk of descent in darkness
Two large transverse crevasses on lower couloir crossed via snow bridges; bridges can collapse or weaken with daily warming cycles
Icefall hazard in lower Curtis Glacier section (600' steep snow 45-70 degrees); significant objective danger from serac and ice chunks on both sides
60-foot section of unprotected 5.0 rock climbing; minimal safety options if climber falls; highly exposed section
Upper Curtis icefall hazard in low visibility; disorientation can lead climbers directly into falling ice zone; critical to stay high and traverse directly below Hourglass
Steep ice sections (60-70 degrees at bottom, 70+ degrees at top) requiring sustained WI3 climbing; ice bulges may be 80+ degrees
Complex route finding on multi-glacier system; summit area routefinding difficult; multiple descent options with different hazard profiles
Grade III terrain with 3300' vertical; sustained elevation and exposure; weather changes create dangerous conditions quickly on open glaciers
Avalanche terrain: Multiple converging avalanche gullies with 30-45 degree slopes. Frequent avalanches on approach and descent routes.
Soft slab avalanche potential: observed avalanche activity between Doctor Creek and Victoria Creek at 5,500 ft on north-facing aspects
Ice quality highly variable: Thin and rotten ice common in transition zones; hollow ice in lower sections; water-spitting holes; melting pillars and detached ice during warm conditions
North-eastern aspect provides late-season ice preservation - best conditions January-February; susceptible to rapid deterioration during warm periods
Temperature-driven transitions from dry to moist snow (moist below 3,500 ft, dry above) - affects both ice quality and avalanche stability
Wind-loaded slopes above: Christmas crust layer (25-35 cm at 4,500 ft) creates hidden instability; variable bonding makes slope stability assessment critical
Rappel descent - verify anchor quality on ice; be aware of loose rock and ice debris below major flows
Route stays in condition longer than southern-facing alternatives due to orientation - check recent Mountain Project trip reports before approach
Raptor closure: Bridge Creek Wall area (1/2 mile buffer) closed January 1 - August 15 for golden eagle nesting
Mixed climbing (M2) requires rock tool placement on ice-covered granite - unpredictable rock ice adherence
Transitional climbing terrain between ice and rock creates anchor placement difficulty
Avalanche terrain: Eightmile Buttress faces east/north - morning ascents recommended to avoid afternoon warming
Soft slab avalanche risk from wind-loaded slopes above (Christmas crust layer creates weak substrate)
Serac hazard from icefall flows above - monitor for rockfall/ice debris
GPS: 47.54823, -120.7518 - area shows recent avalanche activity in NWAC observations
Descent: traverse climber's left, rappel with two 70m ropes to P1 webbing anchor. Alternative: bolt anchors on exposed rock (right), or possibly walk-off upper and far right
Two-pitch route - sustained commitment with limited escape options once committed
Raptor closure: Bridge Creek Wall area (1/2 mile buffer) closed January 1 - August 15 for golden eagle nesting
Thin ice sections - requires careful footwork and ice tool precision
Running water hazard - active water flow creates unpredictable ice conditions
Avalanche approach hazard - access slopes can slide
South-facing aspect: sun-driven melting limits climbing window
Serac/icefall potential from adjacent ice formations
Route-finding difficulty in winter - requires current beta before attempting
